text.title: Weak-Model Preselection Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules grounding the weak-model-preselection methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale.

rule.statement: When input length > 5K tokens AND noise ratio > 70%, a weak model MUST run a filter/classify/extract pass before the strong model receives any of the input.
rule.rationale: Per-token cost ratio 5-30x means the expensive token spend should be on reasoning, not on reading noise.

rule.statement: For high-stakes decisions (medical / legal / security / compliance), the weak model MUST be used as a ranker that surfaces candidates, never as sole gatekeeper that filters them out.
rule.rationale: Weak-model false negatives are catastrophic in regulated contexts; ranker preserves recall while still cutting cost.

rule.statement: Before deployment, the weak model's recall on the filter task MUST be ≥ 0.95 on a labeled eval set of ≥ 100 examples.
rule.rationale: Below 0.95 recall the filter drops relevant items the strong model needs; savings are eaten by accuracy losses.

rule.statement: Each deployed cascade MUST publish a cost-savings ratio (single-pass strong-model cost ÷ cascade total cost) ≥ 3x. Lower ratios indicate the noise ratio doesn't justify the cascade.
rule.rationale: 3x is the empirical break-even after operational overhead (latency, fault budget, deployment complexity).

rule.statement: Cascade MUST expose an `escape_to_full_context` flag: when the weak-model output is below a confidence threshold OR returns nothing, the strong model receives the full original input.
rule.rationale: Without an escape hatch, edge cases silently degrade; the flag turns silent failures into observable ones.
